preamble: Glottal reinforcement ('hard attack') is the insertion of a glottal stop [ʔ] before a vowel-initial syllable. In the strictest reading of this shift, the vowel onset [æ] is replaced by [ʔ] with the vowel fully elided; in its more common form, [ʔ] precedes [æ] rather than replacing it. Both realizations are well-documented across many languages.

examples.note: Glottal reinforcement: [ʔ] is inserted before initial [æ]. In emphatic or careful speech, this can approach full replacement of the vowel onset.

sources: Ladefoged, P. & Johnson, K. (2014). A Course in Phonetics. https://archive.org/details/courseinphonetic00lade
